description:
You are acting as a security auditor who has just received a small JSON-based user-permission manifest that lives in

    /home/user/audit/permissions.json

and its companion JSON-Schema stored at

    /home/user/audit/permissions.schema.json

Your objectives:

1. Using nothing but common CLI tools that are already present in a stock Linux environment (e.g. jq, grep, awk, sed, python), validate that the data file actually satisfies the rules spelled out in the schema.  
   • If every user object contains **all** three required keys – “username”, “uid”, and “permissions” – and the top-level key “users” exists and is an array, consider the file valid.  
   • A simple script or pipeline that counts missing keys via jq is sufficient; you do **not** need an external JSON-Schema engine.

2. Extract every user that fulfils at least one of these “dangerous” conditions:  
   • uid is 0  
   • the “permissions” array contains the literal string “admin”

3. Produce two artefacts in the same directory (/home/user/audit):  

   a) validation.log – **plain-text** file, three (or four) lines, **exactly** in this order and formatting (case-sensitive, colon followed by a single space, no extra whitespace):

      VALIDATION_RESULT: OK|FAIL  
      USERS_TOTAL: <integer>  
      ADMIN_USERS: <comma-separated list with no spaces>  
      (OPTIONAL) ERRORS: <short free-text description>   ← include only when result is FAIL

      Example of a correct *success* file:

      VALIDATION_RESULT: OK  
      USERS_TOTAL: 4  
      ADMIN_USERS: carol  

   b) admin_users.json – a pretty-printed JSON array (indentation produced by jq’s “.” filter is fine) that contains the full objects of every qualifying admin/root user, in the same order they appear in the source file.

4. Nothing else in /home/user/audit should be changed. Do **not** rename or delete any existing file.

Hints (not mandatory):  
• jq can check for key existence with `has("field")`, count objects, and select based on conditions.  
• Python’s json module plus some small logic also works, as long as the output files match the required formats.

When you are done, both files must exist exactly as specified so that an automated test can compare them byte-for-byte.
name: task_000000_bbdacac1
